results.possibleIdentity: Phil Scholz was an engineer active in the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily known for his work in the field of electrical instruments and industrial safety. He was a partner and co-founder of Control Instruments Corporation, established in 1969 in collaboration with his colleague Matthew James "Jim" Schaeffer. Before founding his own firm, Scholz worked at Davis Instrument Company (also known as Davis Instruments) in Livingston, New Jersey, where he served alongside Schaeffer (who was Chief Engineer at Davis). Scholz's technical expertise focused on the design and implementation of electrical instruments for measuring vapors, gases, and air pollution; notably, he and Schaeffer helped commission the first air pollution monitoring station in New York City. His work in the late 1960s and early 1970s aligns with the activities of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as the technical sensors and safety equipment developed by his firms were of the type frequently utilized in the era's cross-disciplinary collaborations between engineers and artists.
